# Code structure mostly reflects core architecture 

| path/name | Role component | Responsibility | Technology | 
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| libs/connector | [**Data Connector**](./modules/connector.qmd) | The "Bridge." It handles authentication and fetches raw data. | BioBlend/API |
| config | [**Configuration Layer**](./modules/configuration.qmd) | **Contains the data contract** for the data AND metadata, plotting defaults. yaml (dictionary) | YAML |
| libs/utils | [**Utilities**](./modules/utils.qmd) | Utility functions used across the application (bridges), such as file config loading and gatekeeping. Contains eg. config loader | Python |
| libs/ingestion | [**Data Ingestion**](./modules/ingestion.qmd) | The "Gatekeeper." It reads CSV/JSON/VCF and ensures the file isn't corrupted. It also apply basic **defensive programming** checks : metadata data and metadata following data contract (#TODO - ? config loader?)- placement of configuration files in correct directory | 
| libs/transformer | [**Data Wrangling**](./modules/wrangling.qmd) | Tidy and wrangle data, applies the data contract for both data and metadata and join metadata in a single tidy dataset | Polars |
| libs/viz_core | [**Visualization Factory**](./modules/visualisation_factory.qmd) | functions to create Plotly object independently of the data. Plotting defaults are passed to the plotting function via configuration files (configuration layer).| Plotly | 
| app/src/server.py | [**Orchestrator Logic**](./modules/dashboard_app.qmd) | This is the shiny server logic that **reacts** to user input and calls the right functions. | Shiny Express |
| app/src/ui.py | [**Display & Interaction**](./modules/dashboard_app.qmd) | The "Face." The HTML/CSS layout and the sliders/dropdowns the user clicks. (Think shiny UI) | Shiny Express |

The major layers of the architecture : Configuration, Data Ingestion, Data Wrangling, Visualization Factory, [#REVIEW Orchestrator Logic, Display & Interaction] are isolated. Bridges are done by utilities. No reference to specific species or data type is hardcoded in the code, but rather defined in the configuration files. This allows for a flexible and extensible architecture, where new species or data types can be added by simply creating new configuration files and ensuring the data follows the defined data contract.


## CodeBase organisation

Each layer is organized in a separate module (except the app that contains both the Controler/Orchestrator Logic and the Display & Interaction layers). 

Each module contains functions related to its specific responsibility, and the code is structured to maintain a clear separation of concerns. 

The configuration files are organized in a dedicated directory, with subdirectories for different types of data (e.g., species, audit) to keep things organized and easily navigable.

#TODO - IMPROVE
Each directory contains a README file that explains the purpose of the module, its main functions, and how it interacts with other parts of the codebase. This documentation is crucial for maintaining clarity and ease of use for future developers who may work on the project.

The required libraries and dependencies for each module are listed in "pyproject.toml" files within that module's directory, allowing for easy setup and installation of the necessary packages. This modular approach not only enhances the maintainability and scalability of the codebase but also allows for easier testing and debugging, as each module can be developed and tested independently before being integrated into the larger application. Currently the modules are part of the same repository, but as they develop, they can be split into separate repositories and added as git submodules to maintain the modular structure while allowing for independent development and versioning of each component.


Major libraries used include:
- Polars for data manipulation in the Transformation layer (Data Wrangling), chosen for its high performance
- plotting library (Altair to allow using grammar of graphics similar to ggplot2). At a layter stage Plotly can be added for interactivity with the Visualization Factory (at a later stage). Note that it is likely possible to add wrapper to use R plotting libraries (eg. ggplot2) in the Viz Factory if needed, but the idea is to keep it in Python for now for simplicity and to avoid adding too many dependencies. 


Oview of the repository structure: 


```text
%%| file: _tree.qmd
``` 

<!-- 
_tree.qmd #TODO - Either actualize comments or remove (reorganized so much more logic)
-->
